1.synthesis: Paul draws the practical consequence of the covenant promises he has just cited: 'Since we have these promises, beloved, let us cleanse ourselves from every defilement of body and spirit, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God' (v.1). The promises of the Father's dwelling, welcome, and ongoing fathering are not simply reassurances but motivations for the pursuit of holiness. He then appeals directly: 'Make room for us in your hearts. We have wronged no one, we have corrupted no one, we have taken advantage of no one. I do not say this to condemn you, for I said before that you are in our hearts, to die together and to live together' (vv.2–3). The appeal concludes with apostolic confidence: he is filled with comfort; overflowing with joy in all their affliction (v.4).
1.historical_context: The section connects the covenantal promises of 6:16–18 directly to an ethical imperative and a personal appeal. The Corinthian situation involved real accusations against Paul: his critics charged him with taking financial advantage and deceiving the community. His threefold denial — wronged no one, corrupted no one, taken advantage of no one (v.2) — echoes Samuel's farewell speech in 1 Samuel 12:3, where Samuel invites the people to bring any charge against him. The language of the 'defilement of body and spirit' (v.1) reflects Second Temple Jewish purity categories applied to the whole person — not merely ritual but moral defilement — and Paul extends them to both bodily and spiritual dimensions of the believer's life.
1.literary_structure: The paragraph serves as both the conclusion of the covenantal warning (6:14–18) and the introduction to Paul's personal account of the Macedonian meeting with Titus. The 'therefore' of v.1 draws directly from the promises of 6:16–18; the appeal of vv.2–4 echoes and continues the appeal of 6:11–13. The section is held together by the paradox of confidence and affliction in v.4: Paul is filled with comfort and overflowing with joy precisely in all his affliction — the very paradox-pattern that characterized the hardship catalogue of 6:8–10.
1.theological_application: Gill on v.1 makes a vital observation about the relationship between the promises and the imperative: the Corinthians 'had not in hope, as Old Testament saints had the promises of the Messiah,' but 'in hand, in actual possession; for God was really become their God and Father, and they were his people and children; they had had communion with him, and were received.' The call to cleanse themselves is therefore not a condition for receiving the promises but a response to promises already enjoyed. This is the grammar of all evangelical holiness: the indicative of actual possession motivates the imperative of practical cleansing. Matthew Henry identifies two kinds of defilement addressed here — 'defilements of the flesh or body' (things that pollute bodily conduct) and 'defilements of the spirit' (pride, covetousness, unbelief, and whatever defiles the inner person) — both requiring the active, ongoing work of cleansing 'in the fear of God.'
1.christology: The promises that ground the holiness imperative are promises fulfilled in Christ: God's dwelling among them, his walking with them, his fatherly reception — all are realized through union with the Son. The 'fear of God' that motivates the cleansing is the reverence appropriate to the one who has accomplished so great a salvation and indwells his people by the Spirit. The confidence Paul expresses in v.4 — comfort amid affliction, joy amid suffering — is a confidence grounded not in circumstances but in the resurrection reality: the same Christ who was comforted in his own suffering and exaltation now comforts those who share his pattern of life.

5.pericope_label: Paul's Joy at the Corinthians' Repentance
5.synthesis: Paul resumes the narrative thread from 2:13, tracing the path of his anxiety. When he came into Macedonia he had no rest — conflicts without, fears within (v.5). But God, who comforts the downcast, comforted him by the arrival of Titus, whose report brought joy: not just that the Corinthians had missed Paul, but that they had mourned, that they had shown zeal for him (vv.6–7). His earlier letter grieved them — and though he regretted writing it, he now rejoices: not that they were made sorry but that they were made sorry 'unto repentance' (v.9). For godly grief produces repentance that leads to salvation without regret; worldly grief produces death (v.10). The paragraph closes with Paul's confidence in them in all things, which he freely confesses (v.16).
5.historical_context: Matthew Henry notes the structural connection: 'There seems to be a connection between ch.2:13 (where the apostle said he had no rest in his spirit when he found not Titus at Troas) and the fifth verse of this chapter.' The narrative of anxiety-then-comfort follows the geography of Paul's mission: Troas → Macedonia → Corinth via Titus. The distinction between 'godly grief' (kata theon lupē) and 'worldly grief' (tou kosmou lupē) in v.10 was meaningful in a culture shaped by Stoic philosophy, which treated grief itself as a vice to be overcome. Paul does not overcome grief but distinguishes productive grief (oriented toward God) from destructive grief (oriented toward loss of worldly goods or status). The seven effects of godly sorrow in v.11 — earnestness, eagerness to clear themselves, indignation, fear, longing, zeal, punishment — describe the moral chemistry of genuine repentance.
5.literary_structure: The paragraph follows a movement from trouble to comfort to rejoicing. The trouble (v.5) is matched by the divine comfort through Titus (v.6); the sorrow of the letter (v.8) is matched by the repentance it produced (v.9); the worldly grief that produces death (v.10) is contrasted with the godly grief that produces salvation. Chrysostom observes that Paul repeatedly 'exalts their praises and showeth their love' — the recounting of Titus's joy at the Corinthians' reception is itself evidence of the depth of their goodwill. The confidence of v.16 closes the account of the reconciliation and paves the way for the collection appeal of chapters 8–9.
5.theological_application: Gill on v.10 supplies a precise account of what makes sorrow 'godly': it 'did not arise from the power of free will, nor from the dictates of a natural conscience, nor from a work of the law on their hearts, or from a fear of hell and damnation, but it sprung from the free grace of God; it was a gift of his grace, the work of his Spirit, and the produce of his almighty power.' Evangelical repentance is therefore not a human moral achievement but a divine gift — which is precisely why it leads to salvation 'without regret.' The person who has been grieved by the Spirit toward God has nothing to regret, because the grief has done its saving work. Gill on v.11 identifies the seven effects of godly sorrow as 'evidences of the sincerity and genuineness of it' — real repentance is always verified by these downstream fruits: diligence to set things right, indignation at the sin that caused offense, fear of God, longing for restored relationship, zeal against the wrong, and willingness to see the discipline through. The Corinthians had shown all seven; Paul can therefore have full confidence in them.
5.christology: Gill on v.6 identifies the 'God who comforts the downcast' as the one who 'has raised comfort to them; they may expect it, and the apostle experienced it; and which he refers to God as the author of it, as he had done in the beginning of this epistle, calling him the God of all comfort.' The comfort came through Titus — a human instrument — but the source was divine. The pattern is the same throughout the letter: God comforts through the human means of apostolic fellowship, reported news, and received love. The repentance of the Corinthians is itself a christological event: their grief was 'unto repentance' (v.9), a turning toward God that the Spirit alone produces — the same Spirit who was poured out through the apostolic proclamation of Christ crucified and risen.
